course.title: Heat and Mass Transfer
course.description: A rigorous, industry-oriented course on heat and mass transfer. Students begin with real engineering problems — cooling a gas turbine blade, insulating a refinery pipe, quenching a steel billet — and work backward to the theory needed to solve them. Applicable to thermal system design, process industries, energy systems, electronics cooling, and manufacturing.

modules.title: Conduction
modules.description: Fundamentals of heat conduction: governing equations, steady-state analysis, extended surfaces, lumped analysis, and transient methods — all grounded in industrial context.

chapters.title: Fundamentals of Heat Conduction and Governing Equations
chapters.description: Before deriving any equations, this chapter asks: where does conduction matter most in industry? From furnace walls to PCB layers to Arctic pipelines, conduction is the invisible bottleneck in thousands of thermal systems. This chapter builds the mathematical tools — Fourier's Law, the heat diffusion equation in three coordinate systems, and boundary conditions — that engineers use daily to answer the question: how hot does it get, and how fast?

lessons.title: Introduction to Heat Transfer and Conduction
lessons.description: Starting from industrial problems to motivate the study of heat transfer modes, with focus on why conduction is the foundational mode.

topics.title: What is Heat Transfer and Why Does it Matter in Industry?

subtopics.title: The Engineering Problem Behind Heat Transfer
subtopics.content:
Every thermal engineering problem starts with one question: will this component get too hot? A gas turbine blade operating in 1600°C combustion gas must survive indefinitely at metal temperatures below 950°C. A lithium-ion battery cell must not exceed 45°C during fast charging or it will degrade. A silicon processor die must stay below 85°C junction temperature or it throttles and fails. These constraints are not thermodynamic constraints — thermodynamics tells us how much heat is involved. They are heat transfer constraints — they tell us how fast the heat must be removed and by what mechanism.

Heat transfer is the engineering science that predicts the rate of thermal energy exchange between bodies at different temperatures. It answers three practical questions that thermodynamics cannot: (1) What is the temperature distribution inside a component? (2) What is the rate of heat flow across a boundary? (3) How long does it take for a component to reach a new thermal equilibrium? Mastering heat transfer means being able to answer these questions for any geometry, material, and operating condition.
examples: Industry — Gas Turbine Blade: A Rolls-Royce Trent 1000 turbine blade (used in Boeing 787) is exposed to combustion gas at ~1600°C, yet the blade alloy melts at ~1300°C. It survives because of a sophisticated heat transfer system: internal serpentine cooling channels carry compressor bleed air (at ~600°C), film cooling holes create a protective air film on the outer surface, and a ceramic thermal barrier coating (TBC, k ≈ 2 W/m·K, 0.1–0.3 mm thick) adds additional insulation. The blade design question is pure heat transfer: given these cooling mechanisms, what is the metal temperature distribution, and where is the hottest spot?

Numerical — Electronics Cooling: A server CPU generates 150 W in a 35×35 mm die area. The heat flux is q'' = 150 / (0.035×0.035) = 122,450 W/m² ≈ 122 kW/m². Compare this to a domestic electric stove hotplate generating ~7 kW/m². The CPU dissipates heat at 17× the intensity of a stove element — from a chip the size of a postage stamp. Without an engineered heat transfer path (thermal interface material → heatsink → fan → ambient), the chip would reach destructive temperatures within milliseconds of powering on.

Industry — Refractory Furnace Design: In an electric arc furnace (EAF) used in steel making, the inner refractory lining (magnesia-carbon bricks) reaches 1700°C on the hot face. The outer steel shell must stay below 200°C to prevent warping. The refractory liner thickness (typically 400–700 mm) is designed using conduction analysis to maintain this outer surface temperature limit while minimizing the amount of expensive refractory material used.

image_meta.caption: Heat conduction through crystal lattice — phonon transfer mechanism
Kettle showing all three heat transfer modes: conduction (base), convection (water), radiation (flame)
Heat flow through a wall — temperature gradient and Fourier's Law

subtopics.title: Three Modes of Heat Transfer — How Industry Uses Each
subtopics.content:
Three distinct physical mechanisms transfer heat. Understanding which mode dominates in a given situation is the first step in any thermal analysis — because the governing equations, material properties, and design strategies differ completely between modes.

**Conduction** transfers heat through a stationary medium by molecular energy exchange (lattice vibrations in solids, molecular collisions in fluids). It dominates inside solid walls, across thin films, and through insulation layers. Key property: thermal conductivity k (W/m·K).

**Convection** transfers heat between a solid surface and a moving fluid. It dominates at surfaces exposed to flowing gases or liquids — engine cooling jackets, heat exchanger tube walls, airflow over electronics. Governed by Newton's Law of Cooling: Q = hA(T_s − T_∞). Key property: convective heat transfer coefficient h (W/m²·K).

**Radiation** transfers heat via electromagnetic waves and requires no medium. It dominates at high temperatures (furnaces, combustion chambers) and in vacuum environments (spacecraft, cryogenic vessels). Governed by Stefan-Boltzmann Law: Q = εσA(T_s⁴ − T_surr⁴). Key property: emissivity ε (dimensionless).

In practice, most industrial systems involve all three modes simultaneously — but one typically controls the thermal resistance of the system. Identifying the dominant mode and controlling resistance is the engineer's primary thermal design task.
examples: Industry — Overhead Power Transmission Line (All Three Modes Simultaneously): A 400 kV ACSR conductor carrying 1000 A generates Joule heat internally at q̇ = ρ_e × J² (W/m³). This heat (1) conducts radially through the aluminum strands to the outer surface, (2) convects from the surface to the ambient wind (h ≈ 15–50 W/m²K depending on wind speed), and (3) radiates from the surface (ε ≈ 0.5 for weathered aluminum). The maximum current the line can carry (its 'ampacity') is determined by setting the conductor temperature to its thermal limit (75–90°C) and solving the steady-state balance between heat generation and the sum of convective + radiative losses. Grid operators use this calculation in real time to manage line loading.

Numerical — Identifying the Controlling Resistance: A furnace wall has inner surface at 900°C and outer surface at 60°C. The wall is 200 mm thick firebrick (k = 1.0 W/m·K) with an outer convection coefficient h = 10 W/m²K to ambient air at 30°C. Conduction resistance: R_cond = L/kA = 0.2/(1.0×1) = 0.2 K/W. Convection resistance: R_conv = 1/hA = 1/(10×1) = 0.1 K/W. Total: 0.3 K/W. Conduction controls (67% of resistance). Doubling the insulation thickness (to 400 mm) would reduce total resistance to 0.5 K/W and cut heat loss by 40% — a clear economic benefit quantified by heat transfer analysis.

Industry — Solar Panel Thermal Management: A photovoltaic panel in Chennai (irradiation ~900 W/m²) absorbs radiation but converts only ~20% to electricity. The remaining 80% (720 W/m²) becomes heat in the silicon cells. The cells lose heat by: convection to wind on the front surface (h ≈ 15 W/m²K), conduction through the back EVA encapsulant and glass, and radiation from front and back surfaces. For every 1°C rise above 25°C, panel efficiency drops by 0.4% (for silicon). A panel at 65°C (40°C above STC) loses 16% of its rated output — a revenue loss quantified using combined-mode heat transfer analysis.

image_meta.caption: Kettle demonstrating conduction, convection and radiation simultaneously
Heat transfer modes in stars — conduction, convection, radiation zones
Earth's overall heat flow — all three heat transfer mechanisms in the global energy system

subtopics.title: Why Conduction is the Foundation — Industry Perspective
subtopics.content:
Even in systems dominated by convection or radiation, there is always a conduction path that must be analyzed first. Consider these industrial realities:

**Every surface has a solid wall.** Heat must conduct through it before convection can carry it away. The thermal resistance of the wall — even a thin one — determines the temperature difference across it and can be the controlling design variable.

**Every contact between components has a conduction interface.** In electronics, the conduction path from silicon die → solder bump → copper trace → PCB → TIM → heatsink involves 8–10 distinct material layers, each with its own conduction resistance. The weakest (highest resistance) layer sets the junction temperature.

**Conduction governs component life.** Fatigue, creep, oxidation, and phase change in materials are all exponentially sensitive to temperature. The temperature at the critical location — the hottest point — is determined by solving the conduction equation with appropriate boundary conditions.

**Mass transfer mirrors conduction.** The mathematical structure of Fick's Law of mass diffusion is identical to Fourier's Law of heat conduction. Engineers who master conduction analysis can immediately apply the same framework to diffusion problems in Unit V — a major time-saving insight.
examples: Industry — Thermal Interface Material (TIM) in Electric Vehicle Battery Packs: In a Tesla Model 3 battery module, each 21700 cell is surrounded by a thermally conductive gap filler (k ≈ 3–5 W/m·K). The module cooling plate beneath conducts heat to the glycol-water coolant channels. Even though the dominant heat removal mechanism is ultimately liquid convection, the conduction path through the TIM controls the cell-to-plate temperature difference. If TIM conductivity drops from 5 to 1 W/m·K (due to aging or poor assembly), cell temperature rises by ~8°C at full charge rate — directly reducing battery life by 15–20% (Arrhenius degradation model). This is a pure conduction design problem.

Numerical — Thin Wall Dominance: A heat exchanger tube wall (steel, k=50 W/m·K) is 3 mm thick. Conduction resistance per unit area: R_wall = L/k = 0.003/50 = 6×10⁻⁵ m²K/W. Typical convection resistances on each side: R_conv = 1/h ≈ 1/5000 = 2×10⁻⁴ m²K/W (liquid side). The wall resistance is 3× smaller than each convection resistance — confirming that in liquid-to-liquid heat exchangers, the tube wall itself is thermally transparent, and improving the convection coefficients (e.g., by adding turbulence promoters) delivers far more benefit than using a more conductive tube material.

Industry — Permafrost Thaw Under Arctic Pipelines: The Trans-Alaska Pipeline System (TAPS) carries crude oil at 60–80°C through 1,300 km of permafrost terrain. If heat conducts from the hot pipe to the frozen ground, the permafrost thaws, ground subsidence occurs, and the pipeline buckles. TAPS uses vertical support members (VSMs) with heat pipes to prevent ground warming, and buries sections with insulation. The design of the insulation system — thickness, material, geometry — is determined by solving the steady-state cylindrical conduction equation in the soil domain, ensuring the freeze-thaw boundary (0°C isotherm) remains outside a safe radius from the pipe over the pipeline's 30-year design life.

image_meta.caption: Fourier's law of heat conduction — temperature gradient drives heat flow through a wall
Thermal resistance analogous to electrical resistance — circuit analogy for composite walls
Heat balance on a differential control volume — foundation of the heat diffusion equation

subtopics.title: Fourier's Law — From Industrial Observation to Mathematical Form
subtopics.content:
Jean-Baptiste Joseph Fourier observed in 1822 what every foundry worker had experienced empirically for centuries: thicker walls mean slower heat loss; more conductive materials mean faster heat flow; steeper temperature differences drive more heat. Fourier formalized this into the most important equation in conduction heat transfer.

**Fourier's Law (1D Cartesian form):**
$$q'' = -k \frac{dT}{dx} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

**Total heat transfer rate through area A:**
$$Q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx} \quad \text{(W)}$$

**Vector (3D) form:**
$$\vec{q''} = -k \nabla T$$

where:
- $q''$ = heat flux (W/m²) — power per unit area
- $Q$ = heat transfer rate (W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K) — the material's ability to conduct heat
- $dT/dx$ = temperature gradient (K/m) — the driving force
- The **negative sign** ensures heat flows in the direction of decreasing temperature (from hot to cold) — consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics

**Industrial implication:** Every insulation design, every wall thickness calculation, every heat loss estimate in a factory begins with this equation. It is not abstract — it is the daily tool of thermal engineers.
examples: Industry — Furnace Insulation Heat Loss Audit: A 100 m² section of rotary kiln shell (used in cement manufacturing) has a refractory lining of 230 mm (k = 1.1 W/m·K). Inner surface temperature is measured by pyrometer at 1100°C; outer surface is at 180°C. Using Fourier's Law: q'' = -k(dT/dx) = -1.1 × (180-1100)/0.230 = -1.1 × (-4000) = 4400 W/m². Total heat loss: Q = 4400 × 100 = 440 kW. At an electricity cost of ₹8/kWh, this represents ₹42,240/day in lost energy — purely from conduction through the kiln shell. Adding 50 mm extra insulation (k=0.2 W/m·K) would save approximately ₹18,000/day. This is Fourier's Law applied directly to plant economics.

Numerical — Copper vs. Stainless Steel Heat Spreader: Two heat spreader plates of identical thickness (5 mm) and area (100×100 mm) are compared for an electronics cooling application. Under the same temperature gradient of 200°C/m: For copper (k=385 W/m·K): q'' = 385×200 = 77,000 W/m². For stainless steel (k=16 W/m·K): q'' = 16×200 = 3,200 W/m². Copper spreads heat 24× more effectively than stainless steel. This explains why high-performance GPU heatsinks use copper vapor chambers (effective k > 10,000 W/m·K) while budget products use aluminum (k=237) or steel.

Industry — Cold Chain Validation (Pharmaceutical Logistics): A refrigerated vaccine container wall is 80 mm thick polyurethane foam (k=0.023 W/m·K). External ambient is 40°C; internal target is 2–8°C. Using worst case (2°C inside): q'' = 0.023×(40-2)/0.08 = 0.023×475 = 10.9 W/m². For a container with 1.2 m² total wall area: Q = 10.9×1.2 = 13.1 W. The refrigerant ice pack (latent heat ~334 kJ/kg) must absorb this load: for a 48-hour transit, total heat ingress = 13.1×48×3600 = 2.26 MJ → requires at least 6.8 kg of ice. This is a direct Fourier's Law calculation that determines cold chain packaging design and regulatory compliance.

image_meta.caption: Fourier's law of heat conduction — q'' = -k·dT/dx through a flat wall
Crystal lattice heat conduction — phonon energy transfer from hot to cold
Temperature gradient and heat flux direction through a composite wall

subtopics.title: Thermal Conductivity — Physical Mechanisms and Engineering Values
subtopics.content:
Thermal conductivity k is not just a number in a table — it is the macroscopic signature of how energy moves at the molecular level. Understanding its physical origin helps engineers predict how k changes with temperature, composition, and microstructure — and how to engineer it.

**In metals:** Heat is carried primarily by free electrons. Pure metals (Cu, Al, Ag) have high k. Alloying reduces k by electron scattering at impurity sites — pure copper (k=385) vs. brass Cu-Zn alloy (k=110). Cold working and grain boundaries further reduce k.

**In non-metallic solids (ceramics, polymers):** Heat is carried by phonons (quantized lattice vibrations). k is much lower. Dense, crystalline ceramics (alumina, k=30) are more conductive than porous or amorphous ones (firebrick, k=1.0).

**In gases:** Molecular collision frequency determines k. k is low (air: 0.026 W/m·K) and increases with temperature (unlike metals). Still air trapped in pores is why insulation materials work — they suppress convection while low gas k limits conduction.

**In fluids:** k is intermediate, dominated by molecular diffusion. Water (k=0.6) is 23× more conductive than air — which is why water-cooled systems are far more compact than air-cooled ones.

**Temperature dependence:** For most engineering purposes over moderate temperature ranges:
$$k(T) = k_0(1 + \beta T)$$
For metals: k decreases with T (more phonon scattering). For most non-metals and gases: k increases with T.
examples: Industry — Why Heat Pipes are Used in Laptops: A 6mm copper/water heat pipe has an effective thermal conductivity of 10,000–100,000 W/m·K — hundreds of times higher than solid copper. This is achieved not by material magic but by using the latent heat of vaporization: water evaporates at the hot end (processor), vapor flows to the cold end (heatsink), condenses, and wicks back. The laptop industry shifted to heat pipes in the 1990s because they achieve the same spreading effect as a copper bar 100mm thick but weigh only a few grams. Understanding that 'apparent k' is not just a material property but depends on the transport mechanism is a crucial engineering insight.

Numerical — Effect of Alloying on Furnace Heating Rate: A tool steel billet (k=36 W/m·K) and a pure iron billet (k=73 W/m·K) of identical dimensions are placed in a furnace. During heating, the temperature gradient at the surface is the same for both (same furnace temperature, same surface heat flux condition). However, the heat penetrates twice as fast into the iron billet because of its higher k. The thermal diffusivity α = k/(ρcₚ): for pure iron α = 73/(7870×452) = 2.06×10⁻⁵ m²/s; for tool steel α = 36/(7800×460) = 1.00×10⁻⁵ m²/s. The iron billet reaches thermal equilibrium (center temperature = furnace temperature) in approximately half the time. This affects furnace productivity scheduling directly.

Industry — Aerogel Blankets in LNG Storage: Cryogenic LNG tanks (storing liquid natural gas at -162°C) require insulation with the lowest possible k. Conventional polyurethane foam (k=0.023) requires 300–400 mm thickness to meet boil-off rate specifications. Aerogel blankets (k=0.013 W/m·K at -100°C, even lower at cryogenic temperatures) achieve the same insulation with 100–150 mm. On a 150,000 m³ LNG carrier, reducing insulation thickness from 350mm to 150mm saves ~800 tonnes of insulation weight — a direct fuel cost and cargo capacity benefit. The ultra-low k of aerogel comes from its 90–99% air-filled nanoporous structure where pore sizes (<100 nm) are smaller than the mean free path of air molecules, suppressing even gaseous conduction.

image_meta.caption: Atomic-level mechanism of heat conduction in a crystal — phonon propagation
Thermal resistance circuit — electrical analogy showing series resistances
Heat transfer modes comparison — conduction vs. convection vs. radiation

subtopics.title: Thermal Resistance — The Engineer's Primary Design Tool
subtopics.content:
The thermal resistance concept transforms Fourier's Law into a circuit analogy that makes complex multi-layer conduction problems intuitive and solvable by inspection. It is the single most practically useful concept in this entire chapter.

**Definition:**
For steady-state 1D conduction, rearranging Fourier's Law:
$$Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}} \quad \text{where} \quad R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA} \quad (\text{K/W})$$

This is mathematically identical to Ohm's Law ($I = V/R$):
| Electrical | Thermal |
|---|---|
| Current $I$ (A) | Heat flow $Q$ (W) |
| Voltage $\Delta V$ (V) | Temperature difference $\Delta T$ (K) |
| Resistance $R$ (Ω) | Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ (K/W) |

**For series layers (composite wall, same Q through each):**
$$R_{total} = \sum_i \frac{L_i}{k_i A}$$

**For parallel layers (same ΔT across each):**
$$\frac{1}{R_{total}} = \sum_i \frac{k_i A_i}{L_i}$$

**Including surface convection resistances:**
$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{hA}$$

**Total wall thermal resistance (wall + both convection boundaries):**
$$R_{total} = \frac{1}{h_1 A} + \frac{L}{kA} + \frac{1}{h_2 A}$$

The layer with the **highest $R_{th}$** is the controlling resistance — improving it gives the maximum reduction in heat flow (or maximum reduction in temperature rise).
examples: Industry — Building Energy Audit (R-value in Construction): ASHRAE and NBC (National Building Code of India) specify insulation requirements using R-values (R = L/k in m²K/W, without area). A typical Chennai commercial building wall: 13mm gypsum board (k=0.16) + 100mm AAC block (k=0.20) + 13mm external plaster (k=0.72). R_gypsum = 0.013/0.16 = 0.081 m²K/W; R_AAC = 0.1/0.20 = 0.500 m²K/W; R_plaster = 0.013/0.72 = 0.018 m²K/W. Total R_wall = 0.599 m²K/W. Adding 50mm XPS insulation (k=0.035): R_XPS = 0.05/0.035 = 1.43 m²K/W → R_total = 2.03 m²K/W — a 3.4× improvement in thermal resistance and a proportional reduction in air-conditioning load.

Numerical — Finding Interface Temperature in a Composite Wall: A steam pipe wall has two layers: 5mm steel (k=50 W/m·K) and 50mm mineral wool insulation (k=0.04 W/m·K). Inner surface temperature = 250°C, outer surface = 40°C. Per unit area: R_steel = 0.005/50 = 10⁻⁴ m²K/W; R_insul = 0.050/0.04 = 1.25 m²K/W. Since R_steel << R_insul, virtually the entire ΔT (210°C) drops across the insulation. Heat flux: q'' = (250-40)/(10⁻⁴+1.25) = 210/1.2501 = 167.99 W/m². Interface temperature: T_interface = 250 - q''×R_steel = 250 - 167.99×10⁻⁴ ≈ 249.98°C ≈ 250°C. The steel wall has negligible temperature drop — confirming steel is thermally irrelevant here.

Industry — Thermal Resistance in Microelectronics Package: An IGBT power module used in a 100 kW EV inverter has the following thermal stack (per device): Silicon die → solder → DCB ceramic → solder → copper baseplate → TIM → heatsink. Junction-to-heatsink thermal resistance (specified in datasheet): R_jh = 0.12 K/W. At 300 W power dissipation per switch: T_junction - T_heatsink = 300 × 0.12 = 36°C. If heatsink is maintained at 70°C by liquid cooling, T_junction = 106°C — within the 150°C limit. If coolant fails and heatsink rises to 110°C: T_junction = 146°C — dangerously close to limit. The R_th value from the datasheet is the direct tool engineers use to size the cooling system.

image_meta.caption: Thermal-electrical resistance analogy — the most powerful tool for composite wall analysis
Heat flow through a wall — showing temperature drop across each layer
Fourier's law applied to a plane wall — basis of thermal resistance concept

lessons.title: General Heat Diffusion Equation — Derivation and Physical Interpretation
lessons.description: Deriving the governing partial differential equation for heat conduction from first principles, then connecting each term to industrial scenarios.

topics.title: Energy Balance on a Differential Control Volume

subtopics.title: Why We Need a Differential Equation — The Limitation of Simple Formulas
subtopics.content:
Fourier's Law tells us the heat flow rate when we already know the surface temperatures and the temperature gradient is uniform. But in real industrial components, the temperature is not linear — it varies in three dimensions and changes with time. Consider:

- A turbine blade: temperature varies across its thickness, span, chord, and through time during start-up and shutdown.
- A welded joint: temperature during welding is 3D and highly transient — it determines the heat-affected zone (HAZ) microstructure and residual stresses.
- A PCB: heat sources (chips) are localized; the temperature distribution across the board is non-uniform and must be mapped to find hot spots.

For all these cases, we need a **field equation** — one that gives us the temperature at every point $(x, y, z)$ and every instant $t$. This is the **heat diffusion equation** (also called the heat conduction equation). It is derived by applying the First Law of Thermodynamics to an infinitesimally small control volume inside the conducting medium, then using Fourier's Law to express the conduction terms.
examples: Industry — Weld Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) Prediction: In MIG welding of 25mm AISI 4340 steel plate, the heat input is ~2 kJ/mm. The temperature at the weld pool center reaches >1500°C (melting), while 5 mm away it may be 700°C (austenitizing), and 15 mm away only 300°C (below tempering temperature). The HAZ — the region that undergoes microstructural change — is determined entirely by the transient temperature field T(x,y,z,t). Solving the heat diffusion equation (numerically, using FEM tools like ANSYS or Simufact) allows welding engineers to predict and control HAZ width, cooling rates, and ultimately weld toughness without physical trial-and-error.

Numerical — Identifying Why 1D is Insufficient: A 10mm×10mm chip generates 5W uniformly. Using 1D analysis (heat flows only downward through the PCB): q'' = 5/(0.01×0.01) = 50,000 W/m². For a 1.6mm FR4 PCB (k=0.3 W/m·K): ΔT = q''×L/k = 50000×0.0016/0.3 = 267°C — clearly wrong and physically impossible. The real answer requires 2D/3D conduction analysis, because heat spreads laterally through the copper planes in the PCB, dramatically reducing the thermal resistance. The spreading resistance in 2D/3D is only captured by the multi-dimensional heat diffusion equation.

Industry — Injection Mold Cooling Channel Design: In plastic injection molding, cooling time accounts for 60–70% of the total cycle time. The mold must remove heat from the hot plastic (200–300°C) as fast as possible while keeping mold steel below 60°C to ensure dimensional accuracy. The temperature distribution in the mold steel — governed by the 3D transient heat diffusion equation — determines where cooling channels should be placed, how many are needed, and what coolant flow rate is required. Conformal cooling channels (3D-printed to follow the part contour) are designed entirely from heat diffusion solutions and can reduce cycle time by 20–40%.

image_meta.caption: Energy balance on a differential control volume — first step in deriving the heat diffusion equation
Fourier's law — the building block used in deriving the heat equation
Phonon heat conduction in crystal lattice — molecular basis for the PDE

subtopics.title: Deriving the Heat Diffusion Equation in Cartesian Coordinates
subtopics.content:
Consider a differential control volume of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ within a conducting solid. Apply the energy balance:

$$\dot{E}_{in} - \dot{E}_{out} + \dot{E}_{gen} = \dot{E}_{stored}$$

**Step 1 — Conduction in x-direction:**
Heat conducted into left face: $Q_x = -kA\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_x = -k(dy\,dz)\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}$

Heat conducted out of right face (Taylor expansion):
$Q_{x+dx} = Q_x + \frac{\partial Q_x}{\partial x}dx = -k(dy\,dz)\frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)dx\,dy\,dz$

Net conduction in x: $Q_x - Q_{x+dx} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)dx\,dy\,dz$

**Step 2 — Repeat for y and z directions** (identical derivation)

**Step 3 — Add heat generation and storage terms:**
- $\dot{E}_{gen} = \dot{q}\,dx\,dy\,dz$ where $\dot{q}$ = volumetric heat generation (W/m³)
- $\dot{E}_{stored} = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}dx\,dy\,dz$

**Step 4 — Assemble and divide by $dx\,dy\,dz$:**
$$\boxed{\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(k\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(k\frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right) + \dot{q} = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}}$$

**For constant k** (isotropic, homogeneous material):
$$\nabla^2 T + \frac{\dot{q}}{k} = \frac{1}{\alpha}\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

where $\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p}$ is the **thermal diffusivity** (m²/s).

**Physical meaning of each term:**
- $\nabla^2 T$: net rate of heat conducted into unit volume (spatial curvature of temperature field)
- $\dot{q}/k$: heat generated per unit volume, normalized by conductivity
- $(1/\alpha)(\partial T/\partial t)$: rate of temperature rise — how fast the material responds
examples: Industry — Nuclear Fuel Rod Power Distribution: In a PWR nuclear reactor, UO₂ fuel pellets (diameter 8.2mm, k=3.0 W/m·K) generate heat volumetrically from fission: q̇ = 3×10⁸ W/m³ (peak). The heat diffusion equation in cylindrical coordinates (next lesson) gives T_centerline = T_surface + q̇R²/4k = T_surface + (3×10⁸×0.0041²)/(4×3.0) = T_surface + 421°C. Surface temperature is ~600°C (from coolant boundary condition), so T_centerline ≈ 1021°C. The volumetric heat generation term q̇ in the diffusion equation is what links the nuclear physics (power output) to the thermal engineering (fuel temperature). Without this term in the governing equation, the problem cannot even be formulated.

Numerical — Thermal Diffusivity Governs Response Speed: Copper (α=117×10⁻⁶ m²/s) vs. stainless steel (α=3.9×10⁻⁶ m²/s) — ratio ≈ 30×. If a flat surface of each material is suddenly raised in temperature, the thermal penetration depth (depth to which temperature changes appreciably) after time t is δ ≈ √(αt). After 1 second: δ_Cu = √(117×10⁻⁶×1) = 10.8 mm; δ_SS = √(3.9×10⁻⁶×1) = 1.97 mm. A copper thermocouple sheath responds 5.5× faster to temperature changes than a stainless steel one of the same geometry — a practical instrument selection criterion governed by the (1/α)∂T/∂t term.

Industry — Concrete Thermal Mass in Building HVAC: Concrete (α=0.75×10⁻⁶ m²/s, ρcₚ=2×10⁶ J/m³K) has very low thermal diffusivity — it stores heat slowly but also releases it slowly. The storage term ρcₚ(∂T/∂t) in the diffusion equation represents the thermal flywheel effect. A 300mm concrete slab floor absorbs solar heat during the day and releases it at night — a passive thermal mass strategy reducing peak HVAC load. HVAC engineers use transient conduction solutions (from the heat diffusion equation) to determine optimal slab thickness for different climate zones (Coimbatore, Chennai, Delhi). Too thin: no flywheel effect. Too thick: responds too slowly to be useful.

image_meta.caption: Control volume energy balance — in/out/generation/storage terms of the heat diffusion equation
Temperature profile through a wall — the solution of the 1D Laplace equation
Earth's heat flow — illustrating thermal diffusivity effects at a global scale

subtopics.title: Special Forms of the Heat Diffusion Equation — Industrial Decision Guide
subtopics.content:
The general heat diffusion equation contains all possible conduction scenarios. In practice, engineers apply it in simplified forms depending on the specific physical situation. Choosing the correct simplification before solving is the first critical engineering judgment in any conduction problem.

**Decision tree for simplification:**

**Is the problem steady-state?** (temperatures not changing with time → ∂T/∂t = 0)
- Yes + No heat generation → **Laplace equation:** $\nabla^2 T = 0$
  - *Industrial use: pipe walls, building walls, heat exchanger tube walls*
- Yes + Heat generation → **Poisson equation:** $\nabla^2 T + \dot{q}/k = 0$
  - *Industrial use: current-carrying conductors, nuclear fuel, chemical reactor walls*

**Is the problem transient?** (temperatures changing with time)
- No heat generation → **Fourier (diffusion) equation:** $\nabla^2 T = (1/\alpha)\partial T/\partial t$
  - *Industrial use: quenching, start-up/shutdown transients, thermal cycling*
- With heat generation → **Full equation** (all four terms)
  - *Industrial use: pulse laser heating, resistance spot welding, battery charging/discharging*

**Boundary conditions required (3 standard types):**
- **Type 1 (Dirichlet):** $T_{surface} = T_s$ — specified surface temperature. *Used when surface temperature is measured or controlled directly.*
- **Type 2 (Neumann):** $-k(\partial T/\partial n) = q''_s$ — specified heat flux. For insulated surface: $\partial T/\partial n = 0$. *Used when heat flux is known (e.g., from a heater element).*
- **Type 3 (Robin/Convection):** $-k(\partial T/\partial n) = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ — convection at surface. *The most common industrial BC — almost every exposed surface loses heat by convection.*
examples: Industry — Identifying the Correct Equation Form for Each Plant Scenario: (a) Steady-state heat loss from a buried steam condensate pipe: Laplace equation in cylindrical coordinates — no heat generation, steady operation. (b) Resistance spot welding electrode heating during a 200ms weld cycle: Full transient equation with volumetric heat generation q̇ = ρ_e J² — transient (200ms cycle) and strong Joule heating. (c) Cooling a freshly cast aluminum alloy ingot after solidification: Fourier equation — transient cooling, no internal heat generation (solidification is complete). (d) UO₂ pellet in steady-state reactor operation: Poisson equation in cylindrical coordinates — steady state with nuclear fission heat generation. Each scenario requires a different simplification of the same parent equation.

Numerical — Applying Type 3 Boundary Condition (Most Common Industrial Case): A steel mold plate (k=36 W/m·K) has its back surface maintained at T₀=200°C (by a heater — Type 1 BC). Its front surface is cooled by water spray (h=1500 W/m²K, T_water=20°C — Type 3 BC). At steady state (Laplace equation), for a 20mm thick plate: Q = (T₀ - T_water)/(L/k + 1/h) = (200-20)/(0.020/36 + 1/1500) = 180/(5.56×10⁻⁴ + 6.67×10⁻⁴) = 180/1.222×10⁻³ = 147,300 W/m². Front surface temperature: T_s = T_water + Q/h = 20 + 147300/1500 = 20 + 98.2 = 118.2°C. The Type 3 BC links the surface temperature to the heat flux — neither is known in advance, but both are found simultaneously.

Industry — Thermal Cycling Failure of Electronic Assemblies: In automotive electronics exposed to engine bay temperatures, components cycle between -40°C (cold start) and +125°C (fully warmed) daily. This is a transient problem (Fourier equation) with no heat generation, but the repeated thermal cycles cause differential thermal expansion between materials (mismatch in CTE). The thermal strain depends on how fast the temperature changes — which depends on the transient temperature distribution from the heat diffusion equation. IPC-9701 qualification testing uses thermal cycle counts to failure, which are predicted using FEM models built on the Fourier equation. Engineers use this to select solder alloys, underfill materials, and component spacing to maximize cycle life.

image_meta.caption: Fourier's law — foundation for selecting the correct simplified heat equation form
Thermal resistance network — equivalent to solving the Laplace/Poisson equation in 1D
Energy balance on control volume — showing boundary condition types (in/out/generation)

topics.title: Heat Diffusion Equation in Cylindrical Coordinates

subtopics.title: Why Cylindrical Coordinates — Industrial Geometries
subtopics.content:
The vast majority of process industry components are cylindrical: pipes, tubes, cylinders, annuli, rods, cables, and heat exchanger shells. Solving the Cartesian heat equation for these geometries would require complex domain transformations. The cylindrical coordinate system $(r, \phi, z)$ naturally fits these geometries, simplifying both the governing equation and its boundary conditions.

**General heat diffusion equation in cylindrical coordinates:**
$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(kr\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial \phi}\left(k\frac{\partial T}{\partial \phi}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(k\frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right) + \dot{q} = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}$$

**Most common simplification — long cylinder, radial symmetry, steady-state, no heat generation:**
$$\frac{d}{dr}\left(r\frac{dT}{dr}\right) = 0$$

Integrating twice: $T(r) = C_1 \ln(r) + C_2$ → **logarithmic temperature profile**

This logarithmic profile (not linear like a flat wall) has a critical engineering implication: the inner layers of a cylindrical insulation carry higher heat flux than the outer layers per unit area, because the same total heat Q flows through progressively larger areas as r increases.

**Thermal resistance for a hollow cylinder:**
$$R_{cyl} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} \quad \text{(K/W)}$$

For composite cylinders (pipe + multiple insulation layers) in series:
$$R_{total} = \sum_i \frac{\ln(r_{i+1}/r_i)}{2\pi k_i L}$$
examples: Industry — Refinery Process Pipe Insulation Design (Hot Insulation): A 6-inch (152mm OD) carbon steel steam pipe (k=50 W/m·K, wall thickness 8mm) carries steam at 300°C. Two insulation layers: calcium silicate (r₂=76mm to r₃=126mm, k=0.09 W/m·K) + aluminum cladding (1mm, k=237 — negligible resistance). Outer surface convection to ambient (h=12 W/m²K, T_amb=35°C). Per metre length: R_steel = ln(76/68)/(2π×50×1) = 0.00222 K/W; R_CaSil = ln(126/76)/(2π×0.09×1) = 0.900 K/W; R_conv = 1/(12×2π×0.126×1) = 0.105 K/W. Total R = 1.007 K/W. Heat loss per metre: Q = (300-35)/1.007 = 263 W/m. Over a 500m pipe run: Q_total = 131.5 kW. At ₹6/kWh this is ₹18,936/day in heat loss. Adding another 50mm CaSil layer reduces Q to ~140 W/m — saving ₹9,900/day — justifying the insulation investment in ~6 months.

Numerical — Temperature at Insulation-Pipe Interface: Using the above problem, the steel pipe-insulation interface temperature: T_interface = T_steam - Q × R_steel = 300 - 263 × 0.00222 = 300 - 0.58 ≈ 299.4°C. This confirms the steel wall is thermally transparent (0.58°C drop). The outer CaSil surface: T_outer_CaSil = T_ambient + Q × R_conv = 35 + 263 × 0.105 = 35 + 27.6 = 62.6°C — comfortably below the personnel protection limit of 60°C (with small margin suggesting slightly more insulation is warranted for compliance with BS EN ISO 23551 or similar standard).

Industry — Critical Radius of Insulation (Counter-intuitive Result): For a thin bare electrical cable (r₁=3mm, k_insul=0.15 W/m·K, h=10 W/m²K in still air), adding insulation initially INCREASES heat loss until the insulation outer radius reaches the critical radius r_c = k/h = 0.15/10 = 15mm. Below r_c, the decrease in convection resistance (larger outer area) outweighs the increase in conduction resistance — adding insulation makes the cable hotter, not cooler. Above r_c, additional insulation properly reduces heat loss. This critical radius phenomenon is exploited in the design of electrical cable jacketing (ensuring cables don't overheat) and is catastrophic if ignored in thin-wire applications like thermocouple extension cables.

image_meta.caption: Cylindrical coordinate system (r, φ, z) — the natural system for pipe and annular geometry
Concentric tube cross-section — temperature distribution in cylindrical coordinates
Simple tube temperature profile — T(r) = C₁·ln(r) + C₂ logarithmic distribution

topics.title: Heat Diffusion Equation in Spherical Coordinates

subtopics.title: Spherical Geometry in Industry — Tanks, Pellets, and Particles
subtopics.content:
Spherical geometry appears in industrial applications where pressure vessels need minimum surface area for a given volume (LPG spheres, cryogenic dewars), where particles and pellets are processed (catalytic reactors, food drying, pharmaceutical granulation), and where radiation heat transfer involves hemispherical enclosures.

**1D radial heat diffusion equation in spherical coordinates (steady-state, radial symmetry):**
$$\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2\frac{dT}{dr}\right) + \frac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$$

**With no heat generation, integrating:**
$$T(r) = -\frac{C_1}{r} + C_2$$
This is a **hyperbolic** (1/r) temperature profile — steeper near the center, flatter at large r.

**Thermal resistance for a spherical shell:**
$$R_{sph} = \frac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)$$

**Solid sphere with uniform heat generation (e.g., catalytic particle, nuclear pellet):**
$$T(r) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}}{6k}(R^2 - r^2)$$
$$T_{max} = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}R^2}{6k}$$

The $1/6$ factor (vs. $1/4$ for cylinder, $1/2$ for plane wall) shows spheres are the most efficient geometry for dissipating volumetric heat — heat can escape radially in all directions simultaneously.
examples: Industry — Cryogenic LPG Sphere (Spherical Shell Conduction): A 1500-tonne LPG sphere (liquid propane at -42°C, r₁=8m) has a 20mm steel shell (k=50 W/m·K) and an outer 150mm polyurethane foam jacket (k=0.025 W/m·K, r₂=8.02m, r₃=8.17m). R_steel = (1/8.0 - 1/8.02)/(4π×50) = (0.00031)/(628.3) = 4.9×10⁻⁷ K/W — negligible. R_PUF = (1/8.02 - 1/8.17)/(4π×0.025) = (0.01152)/(0.3142) = 0.0367 K/W. For ambient at 45°C: Q = (45-(-42))/0.0367 = 87/0.0367 = 2,371 W total heat ingress into the sphere. This corresponds to ~7 kg/hr of LPG boil-off, which must either be re-liquefied or flared — a direct operating cost determined by spherical shell conduction analysis.

Numerical — Catalyst Pellet Maximum Temperature: In a packed-bed catalytic converter (automotive exhaust), alumina catalyst pellets (spherical, R=3mm, k=2 W/m·K) carry an exothermic catalytic reaction with q̇=5×10⁶ W/m³ heat generation. T_surface = 400°C (from flowing exhaust gas). T_max = T_s + q̇R²/6k = 400 + (5×10⁶ × 0.003²)/(6×2) = 400 + 3.75°C = 403.75°C. The centerline is only 3.75°C above surface — the small pellet radius and spherical geometry ensure the internal temperature rise is negligible. This confirms pellets of this size will not cause thermal sintering of the catalyst (which occurs above ~500°C). If pellet radius were increased to 15mm: ΔT = 5×10⁶×0.015²/(6×2) = 93.75°C — significantly hotter, risking catalyst deactivation.

Industry — Spherical Storage Tank Sizing for Hydrogen (Emerging Energy): For green hydrogen storage, spherical pressure vessels are preferred for high-pressure applications (350–700 bar) because the sphere minimizes stress concentration (hoop stress is uniform in a sphere vs. concentrated at cylinder ends). The thermal insulation analysis for a vacuum-jacketed sphere (for liquid H₂ at -253°C) uses the spherical shell conduction equation to determine the vacuum jacket thickness that limits boil-off to <0.5% per day — a key performance metric for hydrogen fuel cell vehicle refueling infrastructure. With evacuated multi-layer insulation (MLI, k_eff≈10⁻⁴ W/m·K), the required insulation thickness is only 50mm for a 1m radius sphere.
